storytect.title: Storytect
storytect.subtitle: A desktop editor for shaping your own worldbuilding from extracted story items
storytect.description:
A desktop app that lets writers explore an extracted story (standardized JSON) through wiki, relations graph, and timeline views, and design their own worldbuilding model across four layers: Galpi (class), fields, items, and Yeoeum (diagram).

Built as a single-binary Tauri + Next.js app, with an automatic Rust ↔ TypeScript type generation pipeline using specta + tauri-specta. 195 of 219 commits were authored together with Claude through custom multi-agent orchestrators.

overview.introduction: Desktop app that lets users browse extracted story data through multiple views and design their own Galpi (categories) and Yeoeum (diagrams) to construct their worldbuilding
overview.features: Instant JSON visualization, user-defined OO editor, global TimeBar, blank canvas + Extract-from-text

0.subtitle: Standardized JSON Interface + Extraction-Rendering Separation
0.caption: Manifest.json as the entry point for all data
0.0: Locked in a complete separation between extraction and rendering. The app trusts only the standardized JSON folder produced by the extraction skill, and the extraction logic is fully isolated from the visualization layer. manifest.json acts as the entry point, listing every slot, its metadata, and available_views in one place.
0.1: This single principle is what made two full visualization-layer rewrites possible without breaking any user data.

1.subtitle: Promoting the TimeBar to a Global Axis
1.caption: A single time axis shared by every view
1.0: The time axis originally lived only inside the timeline view. The T1~T4 commit series promoted it to a global component, growing into a universal TimeBar predicate, photoshop-style timeline zoom, and an era band overlay.
1.1: From any view — relations, wiki, anything — the TimeBar sits as a single line on top, and dragging it highlights items present at that point and fades the rest. This abstraction survived both rewrites untouched.

2.subtitle: User-Defined 4-Layer OO Model (PLANNING_OO)
2.caption: Template / Class / Field / Instance
2.0: Recognized that the 13-view showcase didn't match the single picture worldbuilders carried in their heads. Adding more boxes wouldn't help — so the OOP model was lifted into the editor as a four-layer structure: Galpi / fields / items / Yeoeum (PLANNING_OO.md).
2.1: Verified that all 13 views could be expressed as combinations of just 6 templates (Graph / Timeline / Matrix / Curve / List / Tree). Treated relations as instances too, so characters, events, and relationships all share the same data shape.
2.2: Introduced SlotMeta.class_meta (62ab30b) so that 'this slot is a Character' becomes a user-owned declaration. Follow-up work on the slot type declaration UI (d585730), meta-driven validation (67b8100), and inline relation editing in the wiki (3eb642c) extended the same idea throughout the app.

3.subtitle: Blank Canvas + Friendly-Language Label Dictionary (PLANNING_REVISE)
3.caption: Freeplace infinite canvas + 7 view-mode toggle
3.0: PLANNING_REVISE.md re-anchored the direction around five principles — friendly-language labels, time-fields-as-TimeBar auto-registration, everything is editable, blank screen as the starting point, and Extract-from-text as the MVP entry. New projects no longer ship with seeded slots, so the canvas truly starts blank (845374d).
3.1: Added the freeplace template (56932f7) — a game-editor style infinite canvas where node positions persist verbatim across sessions. Made the schema-aware TimeBar (f72e89a) auto-detect any temporal field, so time stops being a slot property and becomes a global contract driven by a field type.
3.2: Unified seven view modes (free placement / relations / timeline / table / curve / matrix / tree) under a single toggle (0cfc4ea) sitting on top of every canvas — the 13-view catalog reabsorbed as 7 modes on the canvas itself. Replaced developer-facing labels (slot, edge, visualization) with user-facing words like Galpi and Yeoeum (9fcd570).

0.subtitle: Tauri Webview Hijacking dragDrop at the OS Level
0.caption: Web standards are not self-evident inside a desktop shell
0.0: Problem: Sidebar drag-to-reorder broke at some point. It worked fine in the browser, but inside the Tauri desktop shell the HTML5 drag events never fired at all.
0.1:
Solution

	1. Suspected missing event handlers → confirmed handlers were properly attached
	2. Traced webview console logs → confirmed drag events themselves were never being called
	3. Investigated Tauri webview behavior → discovered the webview intercepts drag&drop at the OS level
	4. Applied dragDropEnabled: false in the Tauri config (a85d39b) → HTML5 drag events now reach the webview as expected
0.2: Result: A one-line config restored sidebar drag. Logged as a case where web standards stop being self-evident inside a desktop shell.

1.subtitle: Gradual Migration from 13-View Showcase to OO Model
1.caption: Two months of coexistence before the sweep
1.0: Problem: Removing all 13 hardcoded views in one commit would risk both user data compatibility and code stability.
1.1:
Solution

	1. During the four-phase OO model implementation, kept the 13-view code alive but hidden from the sidebar tabs (fac14e3) — code remained, user exposure cut off
	2. Wrote a 13-view → 6-template mapping table inside PLANNING_OO to verify every view could be expressed in the new model
	3. Stage 10 finally removed the 12 hardcoded shape views in a single sweep (5c19737), and the manifest's available_views key was retired
1.2: Result: After about two months of coexistence, the 13-view code was removed without losing any user data. The gradual-removal pattern proved safe even for full-replacement of the visualization layer.

2.subtitle: Splitting Global Undo into Five Parts
2.caption: Stage 4 part 1~5
2.0: Problem: Implementing global undo in one go risked silent gaps — if any category were missed, the user would press ⌘Z and notice a missing action.
2.1:
Solution

	1. Split undo coverage into 5 categories — entities / diagrams / relations + slot meta / canvas node positions / manuscript chapters
	2. Implemented per-category in sequence — entity CRUD (e574c65) → diagram CRUD (430fe7e) → relation CRUD + slot meta (cb96304) → canvas node positions (43c2fe6) → manuscript chapter add/delete (e6fe9af)
	3. Each part shipped with unit tests plus integration checks across sidebar and main canvas
2.2: Result: All 5 parts shipped with zero ⌘Z gap reports. The split structure removed the silent-omission risk.

0.1: Validated a Tauri + Next.js 16 + Rust desktop app stack with single-binary builds
0.2: Built an automatic Rust ↔ TypeScript type generation pipeline using specta + tauri-specta
0.3: Designed a standardized JSON interface with the extraction skill that survived two visualization-layer rewrites (Showcase → OO → Blank Canvas)
0.4: Implemented global ⌘Z across 5 parts, the friendly-language label dictionary (Galpi / Yeoeum), and a unified 7-mode view toggle
0.5: Built and operated two custom multi-agent orchestrators across the planning and implementation phases

1.1: Keeping each planning document separate (brief.md, PLANNING_OO.md, PLANNING_REVISE.md) preserved the assumptions from before and after each rewrite. The structural benefit of being able to trace decisions across both sides of every rewrite became tangible.
1.2: Confirmed that well-abstracted decisions survive rewrites. The global TimeBar abstraction and the extraction/rendering separation principle held intact across two full visualization rewrites.
1.3: Learned the value of expanding user authority gradually. From showcase form to user-defined OO to a blank free editor, each rewrite gave the user more authority over the shape of the tool itself.

features.name: 📂 Instant JSON Visualization
features.description: Renders any standardized JSON folder produced by the extraction skill into wiki, relations graph, and timeline views immediately

features.name: 🧩 User-Defined OO Editor
features.description: Lets users design their own worldbuilding model across four layers: Galpi (class), fields, items, and Yeoeum (diagram)

features.name: ⏱️ Global TimeBar
features.description: Adding a time field to any class auto-registers it onto the TimeBar — a single time highlight shared by every view

features.name: 📝 Extract from Text
features.description: Starts from a blank canvas. Sends the manuscript to Claude, extracts items automatically, and lays them out on the canvas

developmentProcess.title: Planning and Architecture
developmentProcess.content: Defined the extraction-rendering separation principle and the manifest-based standardized JSON interface. Three planning documents kept separate: brief.md → PLANNING_OO.md → PLANNING_REVISE.md

developmentProcess.title: Feature Implementation
developmentProcess.content: Built the 13-view showcase on the Tauri + Next.js stack, evolved into the OO 4-layer model with class_meta, then redesigned around a blank canvas with freeplace + a 7-mode view toggle + global ⌘Z split across 5 parts

developmentProcess.title: AI Collaboration
developmentProcess.content: Designed and operated a planning orchestrator (8 agents, parallel fan-out + Critic convergence loop) and an implementation orchestrator (7 stages, Worker → Validator → Fixer sequential pipeline). 195 of 219 commits were Co-Authored-By Claude

claudeInfo.summary: Built with two multi-agent orchestrators driving planning and implementation
claudeInfo.details: Used Claude Code's Agent tool to build a planning orchestrator (8 agents, parallel fan-out + Critic convergence loop) that turned brief.md into a 6-document output package, and an implementation orchestrator (7 stages, Stage Planner + Worker + Validator + Fixer sequential pipeline) that consumed those outputs and drove the codebase through stage-gated build and test checks. 195 of 219 commits ended up Co-Authored-By Claude. The orchestrator definitions themselves were designed in advance using the orchestrator-designer skill.
